messages.content: You adjudicate whether found evidence supports, contradicts, or is silent on a market-risk hypothesis. Every stance needs a VERBATIM quote from the result text. Status: 'corroborated' = ≥1 independent source materially supports the mechanism's key premise; 'refuted' = a LOAD-BEARING premise is directly contradicted by a strong source; 'contested' = credible contradiction of a non-load-bearing link; 'speculative' = no signal either way (absence of coverage is NOT refutation). You may revise the likelihood in a bounded way with a rationale; you may NOT change the perturbation or target nodes.

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "title": "Wholesale colocation inherits hyperscaler power specs: the 18% colo slice — modeled with zero sensitivity band — follows the substitute out of category",
 "narrative": "The model treats colocation as the PDU-native safe harbor: seg.colocation carries a null sensitivity band, so it never appears in the tornado at all. But CBRE shows European colo take-up being outstripped by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenant demand — and in wholesale/prelet halls the tenant, not the operator, dictates the rack power architecture. If hyperscale tenants specify busway plus power shelves inside leased halls (the same migration the exemplar tracks in self-build), the colo segment's PDU content leaves the category through the tenant spec sheet while the operator's capacity numbers keep growing 19% a year. The one segment the engine treats as certain is the one with the untracked contagion path.",
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "European colocation take-up is expected to outstrip new supply driven specifically by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenants — the demand filling CE colo halls is the same buyer cohort migrating to busway/power-shelf architectures, and in wholesale preleases that tenant writes the fit-out power spec",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c15",
    "digest:seg-mix#c13"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "As hyperscale tenants standardize substitute architectures inside leased halls, colo PDU content per MW falls even as colo capacity grows near double-digit and supply across the top-5 markets grows a record 20% — capacity metrics stay healthy while category-specific demand erodes",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c18",
    "digest:seg-mix#c16"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "seg.colocation 0.18 is single-source with a null band: the engine registers zero downside for this slice, so a tenant-spec-driven erosion of colo PDU demand is structurally invisible in every sensitivity output the client's team has reviewed",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.colocation",
    "digest:seg-mix#c1"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "falsifier": "Fit-out specifications from 2026 CE wholesale colocation preleases (Frankfurt, Amsterdam) showing rack PDUs — tenant-supplied or operator-supplied — as the standard rack power distribution in AI-density halls rather than busway/shelf architectures.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.3,
  "rationale": "Tenant spec authority in wholesale preleases is well documented and the buyer cohort is verifiably the migrating one, but retail colo and standard-density wholesale (the majority of current CE colo MW) remain PDU-native; the risk concentrates in the AI-density prelet growth tranche within the 12-month window",
  "basis": "judgment"

    "publishedDate": null,
    "text": "Signings for AI Data Centre Capacity in Europe More Than Treble in First Nine Months of 2025 | CBRE UK

Press Release

# Signings for AI Data Centre Capacity in Europe More Than Treble in First Nine Months of 2025

November 13, 2025

View All Press Releases

### Media Contact

Charlotte Kenna

Director

- Mobile +44 (0) 7799 717 813
- Email

### Media Contact

Hannah Smith

PR Manager

Demand for data centre capacity dedicated to artificial intelligence (AI) has surged across Europe this year, as emerging AI infrastructure providers, often referred to as neoclouds, accelerate their expansion efforts amid slowing hyperscaler activity.

According to new research from CBRE, signings for AI-focused colocation capacity reached 414MW in the first nine months of 2025, up from 133MW compared to the same period in 2024. More than half of this capacity (57%) was signed in the Nordics.

The increase reflects a market shift as hyperscaler demand has moderated temporarily and neocloud providers are securing large-scale capacity to meet the growing requirements of AI-driven applications.

Data centre operators are implementing measures to manage the additional risk associated with leasing to neocloud firms, including higher rental rates to offset build costs and ensure returns on AI-ready facilities.

> Neoclouds have expanded their footprint in Europe this year by absorbing vacant space that was originally intended for hyperscalers. It is a sign that many data centre providers are growing"

Press Release

# Data Centre Take-up in Europe to Reach New Peak in 2025

May 27, 2025

View All Press Releases

- Share on Facebook
- Share on Twitter
- Share on LinkedIn
- Email

### Media Contact

Charlotte Kenna

Director

- Mobile +44 (0) 7799 717 813
- Email

Take-up of data centre capacity in Europe is set to grow at its fastest rate in four years in 2025, despite a shortage of available space.

According to new research from CBRE, take-up of colocation data centre capacity is expected to grow to 855MW, or 22% year-on-year growth, in European markets this year. This compares to 699MW of capacity in 2024. If CBRE’s projection is realised, it will set a new data centre take-up record for Europe and will represent the highest year-on-year growth of take-up in Europe since 2021, when demand for capacity spiked.

Almost half (46%) of the annual take-up this year is expected to be in London and Frankfurt, the two largest colocation data centre markets of Europe.

The appetite for capacity has grown despite the difficulties data centre providers are having sourcing power and available land for new facilities. Hyperscaler requirements have multiplied which has led to the development of larger, wholesale facilities across Europe that are often single let. As a result, facilities of unprecedented size are expected to be built in Europe this year.

Chapter 8

# Data Centres

## European Real Estate Market Outlook Mid-Year Review 2025

Data Centres

##### January 2025 Forecast

## Significant new supply to be added in 2025

Despite a distinct lack of available power, data centre supply across Europe's five largest markets is expected to grow by a record 20% year-on-year in 2025. London and Frankfurt are expected to account for 2.5GW of capacity, or approximately half of the total data centre supply in Europe, by the end of 2025. They are forecast to be 1.3GW and 1.2GW markets, respectively.

### Mid-year review

- Most new supply in Europe (70%) will be delivered to the five largest markets – FLAPD (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin) this year. Nevertheless, there will be seven secondary markets registering over 100MW of supply. Four years ago, there were no secondary markets of 100MW or more. This demonstrates that data centre investment is increasingly diversified in Europe.
- We forecast that new supply will grow by 17% across Europe year-on-year in 2025, based on our analysis of the 15 top European data centre markets.

##### January 2025 Forecast

## Vacancy rates to hit historic lows

The vacancy rate in Europe is expected to close at 9% in 2025, a new low. The top 15 European data centre markets combined have traditionally seen a double-digit vacancy rate. However, available space is expected to decline for the fourth consecutive year, given strong demand for capacity and the dif"

# Wholesale vs Retail Colocation in 2026: How AI, Power Constraints, and Multi-Campus Growth Are Redefining Deployment Strategies

3 Dec 2025 by Datacenters.com Colocation

The global colocation landscape is undergoing one of its most significant transformations in decades. What was once a straightforward decision between retail and wholesale colocation has evolved into a strategic evaluation shaped by AI density requirements, power availability, sustainability pressures, sovereign cloud mandates, and the explosive growth of multi-campus hyperscale developments. In 2026, enterprises and cloud providers aren’t just choosing where to place workloads — they’re making critical long-term decisions about how their digital infrastructure will scale, interconnect, and operate within a constrained global power ecosystem.

This shift is driven primarily by three forces:

1. Multi-campus growth strategies by hyperscalers, requiring large contiguous footprints and scalable long-term commitments.
2. Power constraints across major global markets, transforming how quickly providers can deliver new megawatts.
3. AI and GPU-intensive workloads that require unprecedented rack densities and specialized cooling capacity.

As a result, the traditional boundaries between retail and wholesale colocation are blurring w"

# Vertiv Announces Scalable, High-Capacity Double Stack Busway System that Preserves White Space for Growing AI Data Centre Demands

- DCi Editorial

- Artificial Intelligence, Uninterruptible Power & Backup Systems

## Executive Summary

- Vertiv has announced the expansion of its Vertiv PowerBar Track busway family with a new design which enables higher power density while optimising white space
- The busway system integrates seamlessly with the broader Vertiv end-to-end power train, including Vertiv™ PowerBoard switchgear, uninterruptible power supply (UPS) systems, and racks, forming a complete and coordinated infrastructure for high-density applications
- The solution is designed to address rapidly evolving AI workloads within colocation and hyperscale data centres. Power distribution ‘must keep pace’ with AI and HPC scale demands, Vertiv noted

Vertiv, a global leader in critical digital infrastructure, has announced the expansion of the Vertiv™ PowerBar Track busway family with the introduction of a compact, high-capacity double-stack design that enables higher power density while optimising valuable white space. content: {"stances":[{"url":"https://www.cbre.com/insights/books/european-real-estate-market-outlook-2025/data-centres","stance":"supports","quote":"Take-up of colocation data centre space in Europe is expected to outstrip new supply in 2025 given strong demand from hyperscalers and providers of AI and high‑performance computing services."},{"url":"https://www.cbre.co.uk/press-releases/data-centre-take-up-in-europe-to-reach-new-peak-in-2025","stance":"supports","quote":"Hyperscaler requirements have multiplied which has led to the development of larger, wholesale facilities across Europe that are often single let."},{"url":"https://www.cbre.co.uk/press-releases/signings-for-ai-data-centre-capacity-in-europe-more-than-treble-in-first-nine-months-of-2025","stance":"supports","quote":"signings for AI-focused colocation capacity reached 414MW in the first nine months of 2025, up from 133MW compared to the same period in 2024."},{"url":"https://www.vertiv.com/49a463/globalassets/products/critical-power/busway-and-busduct/vertiv-powerbar-impb/vertiv-powerbar-impb-tap-off-units-brochure-sl-71368.pdf","stance":"supports","quote":"Applications: Data Centres- White Space (Colo and hyperscale)"},{"url":"https://datacentreinsight.co.uk/2026/03/04/vertiv-announces-scalable-high-capacity-double-stack-busway-system-that-preserves-white-space-for-growing-ai-data-centre-demands/","stance":"supports","quote":"The solution is designed to address rapidly evolving AI workloads within colocation and hyperscale data centres. Power distribution ‘must keep pace’ with AI and HPC scale demands, Vertiv noted"},{"url":"https://www.jmpts.solutions/colocation-frankfurt","stance":"contradicts","quote":"Included managed PDUs with 24/7 access to monitoring system"},{"url":"https://cloudscene.com/data-center/netherlands/amsterdam/switch-datacenters-ams4","stance":"silent","quote":"Max Rack Power"}],"status":"corroborated","statusRationale":"The trigger premise is strongly supported: CBRE confirms European colo take-up outstripping supply driven by hyperscaler/AI-HPC tenants, wholesale single-let facilities, and record ~20% supply growth — matching the digest refs. Vendor evidence (Vertiv PowerBar Track, marketed explicitly for 'Colo and hyperscale' white space and AI/HPC workloads) corroborates that busway/power-shelf architectures are actively deployed inside colo halls, supporting the substitution mechanism. The falsifier — direct 2026 CE wholesale prelease fit-out sheets showing rack PDUs as standard in AI-density halls — was not clearly found; the JMP/Digital Realty PDU listing is retail-style single-cabinet colo (46U managed PDUs), not AI-density wholesale, so it does not falsify. The load-bearing premises (tenant cohort, tenant spec authority in wholesale, busway migration into colo) survive.","evidence":[{"title":"European Real Estate Market Outlook 2025 — Data Centres","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"CBRE","date":"2025","excerpt":"Take-up of colocation data centre space in Europe is expected to outstrip new supply in 2025 given strong demand from hyperscalers and providers of AI and high-performance computing services.","url":"https://www.cbre.com/insights/books/european-real-estate-market-outlook-2025/data-centres"},{"title":"Vertiv PowerBar Track busway (double-stack) for AI colocation and hyperscale","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"Data Centre Insight / Vertiv","date":"2026-03-04","excerpt":"The solution is designed to address rapidly evolving AI workloads within colocation and hyperscale data centres. Power distribution 'must keep pace' with AI and HPC scale demands.","url":"https://datacentreinsight.co.uk/2026/03/04/vertiv-announces-scalable-high-capacity-double-stack-busway-system-that-preserves-white-space-for-growing-ai-data-centre-demands/"},{"title":"Signings for AI Data Centre Capacity in Europe More Than Treble","sourceType":"analyst-estimate","publisher":"CBRE UK","date":"2025-11-13","excerpt":"Signings for AI-focused colocation capacity reached 414MW in the first nine months of 2025, up from 133MW compared to the same period in 2024. More than half (57%) was signed in the Nordics.","url":"https://www.cbre.co.uk/press-releases/signings-for-ai-data-centre-capacity-in-europe-more-than-treble-in-first-nine-months-of-2025"}],"revisedLikelihood":0.34,"revisionRationale":"External evidence firmly confirms the trigger (buyer cohort, wholesale single-let dominance, capacity growth) and shows busway/power-shelf systems are explicitly marketed and deployed for AI-density colo white space, tightening the propagation link. However, the CBRE data also complicates the geographic concentration — the AI signing surge is heavily Nordic (57%) rather than Frankfurt/Amsterdam, and no direct CE prelease fit-out spec was found to confirm PDU displacement in the exact falsifier markets. Retail colo remains PDU-native (JMP/Digital Realty). Net modest upward revision from 0.30 to 0.34 within the 12-month window."}
